region_of_origin: Malay-Indonesian region (Indonesia, Malaysia, Philippines); widely cultivated in Thailand, Vietnam, and Malaysia; popular seasonal fruit in Singapore
food_subgroup: Fresh tropical fruit — translucent white flesh eaten directly off the seed after removing the hairy red or yellow outer skin

ontology.foodex2_description: Rambutan, fresh fruit — no specific FoodEx2 code confirmed; closest parent is tropical fruits category
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for rambutan confirmed in published FoodEx2 catalogue; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code confirmed for rambutan fruit flesh in available catalogue.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving is approximately 5 rambutan fruits, yielding about 100g of edible flesh after peeling; hawker bags sold at Singapore wet markets and pasar malams typically contain 15–20 fruits, representing 300–400g of flesh.

nutrient_source.title: Rambutan — Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo) and FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) and FAO/INFOODS SEA
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for fresh rambutan edible flesh sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition table. Both are approved primary regional sources under Project Veritas source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Tropical Fruits — University of Sydney GI Database and FAO/INFOODS SEA GI reference values
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~49 for rambutan is referenced from FAO/INFOODS SEA GI data and University of Sydney reference values for tropical fruits. Rambutan is not always listed as a standalone entry; the value is consistent with published tropical fruit GI ranges and is classified as low GI (<55). GL per 100g serving = GI × carbohydrate_g / 100 = 49 × 16.0 / 100 = 7.84, rounded to 7.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (11mg/100g) and low caloric density
hypertension.guidance: Rambutan is low in sodium (11mg/100g) and calories (65 kcal/100g), making it a suitable fruit choice for individuals managing hypertension; no specific sodium concern at normal serving sizes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate-low GI (~49) but high cumulative carbohydrate load from typical social eating patterns
type2_diabetes.guidance: A single 5-fruit portion (100g flesh, GL ~7.8) is manageable for most T2DM patients, but the primary clinical risk is the social eating pattern — Singapore families commonly consume 15–20 fruits per sitting (300–400g flesh, 48–64g carbohydrate, GL 23–31), which transforms a low-GI snack into a high-GL eating event; patients should be counselled to limit intake to 5–8 fruits per sitting.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and moderate Vitamin C antioxidant content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Rambutan contains no saturated fat, no cholesterol, and provides 30mg Vitamin C per 100g, which supports vascular health; it is a heart-friendly fruit choice when consumed in moderate portions as part of a balanced diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vitamin C anti-inflammatory antioxidant supporting collagen synthesis
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Vitamin C (30mg/100g) in rambutan acts as an anti-inflammatory antioxidant and supports collagen synthesis relevant to RA joint tissue; no specific RA concern at normal serving sizes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rambutan (红毛丹 / 紅毛丹) — named from the Malay word 'rambut' meaning 'hair', referring to its distinctive soft red or yellow spines — is one of Singapore's most beloved seasonal tropical fruits, available fresh at wet markets and pasar malams primarily during the May–September peak season. The fruit belongs to the soapberry family (Sapindaceae) and is botanically classified as Nephelium lappaceum. After peeling away the hairy outer skin, the edible portion is a translucent, lychee-like white or pale-pink flesh with a sweet-tart flavour and a smooth inedible seed at the centre. Nutritionally, rambutan is modest: 65 kcal per 100g, 16g carbohydrate (14g sugar), 0.9g protein, 0.2g fat, and 0.9g dietary fibre. It provides a useful 30mg Vitamin C per 100g and small amounts of calcium (22mg), potassium (42mg), and niacin (0.8mg). The glycemic index is approximately 49 (low GI), yielding a glycemic load of ~7.8 per 100g serving. The key clinical consideration for rambutan is not any single nutrient but the eating pattern: rambutans are consumed continuously from a bag, and Singapore families commonly eat 20–30 fruits per person in a single sitting, accumulating 300–400g of flesh and 48–64g of carbohydrate — approaching the glycemic load of a full carbohydrate meal. Five fruits (100g flesh) is a clinically manageable T2DM portion; twenty fruits (400g flesh) is not. Rambutan is vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is native to the Malay-Indonesian region and is widely cultivated in Malaysia, Indonesia, Thailand, Vietnam, and the Philippines.
common_user_queries: how many rambutan can diabetic eat
is rambutan good for high blood pressure

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition table, both approved primary regional sources. GI value of ~49 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database reference values for tropical fruits. FoodOn ontology code confirmed. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. Multilingual names for all required Southeast Asian languages (Indonesian, Thai, Vietnamese) have been added.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for fresh rambutan flesh is not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not explicitly reported in the primary sources consulted. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB/FAO source). saturated_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB/FAO source). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — rambutan is a plant food). vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 (confirmed negligible per HPB/FAO source). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant food contains no B12). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant food, no sun-derived vitamin D in flesh). All core macros (protein_g 0.9, fat_g 0.2, carbohydrate_g 16.0) are non-zero and require no zero-macro justification. FoodEx2 code not assigned as no specific confirmed code for rambutan exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue; closest_parent applied with gap note. SNOMED CT code not assigned; gap note provided. GI value of 49 is an analogous estimate consistent with University of Sydney tropical fruit GI reference range; confidence 0.9.
